facts_disclosed: Says she would never open her own restaurant — too much risk

turns.content: three restaurants closed on St-Laurent this month. Three. And one of them had been open less than a year. This industry is brutal

Three in one month on the same street is rough, even by restaurant industry standards. Montreal's food scene is competitive and the margins are incredibly thin. Do you know what happened with them -- was it financial, foot traffic, or something else?

the usual. Rent too high, not enough covers, probably undercapitalized from the start. The one that lasted less than a year — I actually knew the chef. Good cook. Terrible business person. Sank everything into the buildout and had nothing left for the first slow season

That's one of the most common patterns -- talented chefs who can cook circles around anyone but underestimate the business side. A restaurant isn't just a kitchen; it's a real estate play, a staffing operation, a marketing engine, and a financial management exercise, all at once. Being a great cook is maybe 20% of what makes a restaurant survive.

The undercapitalization issue is especially brutal. You need enough runway to survive at least 12-18 months of operating at a loss while you build your clientele. Most first-time owners budget for the buildout and forget about the valley after opening night.

Did this chef have any partners or investors, or was it all personal savings?

personal savings and a loan. From what I heard he's now deep in debt and back working someone else's line. Which is exactly why I will never open my own place. Ever. People keep asking me — "Nadia, when are you going to open your restaurant?" — like it's the natural next step. Non. Absolutely not

That's a very pragmatic stance, and honestly more chefs should think that way before jumping in. The romantic narrative of "chef opens dream restaurant" sells well on cooking shows, but the reality is that most independent restaurants fail. The financial risk is enormous, especially in a city like Montreal where rent is climbing and the labour market for kitchen staff is brutally tight.

What specifically puts you off about it? Is it purely the financial risk, or is there more to it?

the risk is insane. The failure rate — something like 80% close within five years. Eighty percent. You could be the best chef in the city and still go under because your landlord raises the rent or there's construction on your block for six months or your dishwasher quits on a Friday night. There are a thousand ways to fail and maybe three ways to succeed

The 80% number gets cited a lot and while the exact figure varies by study, the reality is close enough. And you're right that the failure modes are incredibly diverse -- it's not just about the food. Location luck, timing, economic conditions, even what street festival gets cancelled that summer. There are variables completely outside your control.

It sounds like you've watched enough colleagues go through it to have a realistic picture, which is valuable. A lot of chefs operate on passion and optimism without doing the math.

exactly. I've seen it happen to people more talented than me. People who could cook anything, who had the vision, the creativity — and they still went under because they couldn't make payroll in February or their wine costs got out of control. I'd rather be the best sous chef in Montreal than a failed restaurateur drowning in debt

That's a really clear-eyed way to think about it. And there's something to be said for the sous chef role that doesn't get enough respect -- you're doing the creative work, you're leading a team, you're executing at a high level, and you're doing it without the existential financial risk. Someone else handles the lease, the insurance, the accountant, the liquor license renewals.

Do you ever feel pressure from peers or the industry culture to make that leap? Because there's definitely a bias in the restaurant world that treats ownership as the ultimate goal, as if staying in someone else's kitchen is somehow settling.

all the time. People act like being sous chef is a stepping stone, like I'm just waiting for my turn. But it's not a stepping stone for me — it's the job. I love the cooking. I love the line. I love the pressure of service. What I don't love is spreadsheets and supplier negotiations and begging a bank for money. The kitchen is where I belong, not in a back office doing accounting

That's a really important distinction. The skills that make someone a great cook -- precision, creativity, intensity, the ability to perform under pressure -- are completely different from the skills that make someone a successful business owner. Some people have both, but they're rare. And there's no shame in knowing which side of that divide you fall on.

Plus, as a sous chef at a good restaurant, you get to focus purely on craft. Every day is about making food better. An owner spends maybe 30% of their time actually cooking, if that. The rest is operations, HR, financial planning. For someone who's in it for the cooking, ownership can actually take you further from the thing you love.

EXACTLY. Thank you. Mon Dieu, finally someone who gets it. My mother keeps saying "open a restaurant, open a restaurant" and I keep telling her — mama, I don't want to open a restaurant and lose everything. I want to cook. That's it. If I wanted to do business I would have gone to HEC, not culinary school

Your mother probably sees your talent and thinks ownership equals success because that's the visible version of making it in the food world. But success in a kitchen looks different from the outside than it does from inside the industry. You know the real score.

And HEC versus culinary school is a perfect way to put it. You chose your path for a reason. The restaurant industry needs excellent sous chefs who are committed to the role -- not everyone passing through on their way to a vanity project that folds in eighteen months.

With three more closures on St-Laurent this month, the evidence is right there on the street.

right there on the street with a "for lease" sign in the window. Putain. And you know what else — even if by some miracle you survive the first five years, then what? You're chained to the place. No holidays, no sick days, every crisis is YOUR crisis. At least as a sous chef if I need a day off the restaurant doesn't collapse

That's a real quality-of-life consideration that gets overlooked in the ownership dream. An owner is never truly off the clock. The restaurant calls at 3am because the walk-in compressor died. A cook no-shows on a Saturday night and you're the one who has to cover. The plumber needs access on your one day off. It's relentless.

As a sous chef, you carry the weight during service but when you walk out after close, the building is someone else's problem. That boundary matters, especially long-term. Burnout rates among restaurant owners are astronomical, and it's not just the finances -- it's the total consumption of your life by the business.

total consumption is exactly the right phrase. I've seen it. Owners who haven't taken a vacation in three years, who look ten years older than they are. No thank you. Ok I feel better having said all this out loud. Sometimes I just need to remind myself that not wanting to own a restaurant doesn't make me less ambitious. It just means I'm not stupid

It's not a lack of ambition -- it's a different kind of ambition. Ambition to be the best at your craft rather than ambition to put your name on a sign. Both are valid, but only one of them doesn't come with an 80% chance of bankruptcy.

And honestly, the chefs who survive as owners long-term are usually the ones who went in with exactly the kind of clear-eyed realism you're showing. So even your awareness of the risks is a sign you're thinking about your career the right way.

well right now my career involves Valentine's Day service next week which is going to be absolute chaos so I need to go plan instead of ranting about the restaurant mortality rate. Merci for letting me vent

Valentine's Day service -- good luck with that. It'll be intense but that's where you thrive. Go plan, and remember: you chose the kitchen over the back office for a reason. That's not a compromise, it's a conviction. Bonne soirée.
